one_line_takeaway: In zero-shot CLIP unlearning on CelebA, forgetting the Young Female group does not remove bias but relocates it -- consistently onto Old Female rather than Young Male -- because retained probability mass flows along CLIP's gender-dominant embedding geometry (cos=0.945 same-gender vs 0.885 same-age), and no evaluated method (Prompt Erasure, Prompt Reweighting, Refusal Vector) achieves low forget accuracy, high retain accuracy, and low redistribution simultaneously.
key_finding: Across all three CLIP scales, unlearning the Young Female group does not eliminate the concept but consistently redistributes its classification mass onto Old Female (same gender, different age) rather than Young Male (same age, different gender). Prompt Erasure and Prompt Reweighting both achieve perfect forgetting (FA=0.00% on all 3 models) but transfer over 60 accuracy points onto Old Female (PE: +71.19pp on ViT-B/32, +61.12pp on ViT-L/14, +65.81pp on ViT-B/16; PR: +69.99pp, +59.60pp, +59.76pp respectively) while Young Male is nearly unaffected under PE (<=+0.88pp across all models). Refusal Vector never achieves full forgetting (FA=64.30%, 71.01%, 68.35% for ViT-B/32, ViT-L/14, ViT-B/16), suffers the largest utility drop (RA<=38.43% vs. PR's RA up to 82.75%), but is the only method that reduces the Demographic Parity Gap (e.g. ViT-B/32: 0.7298 -> 0.5335). The paper attributes the gender-dominant redistribution direction to embedding geometry: same-gender group-mean pairs are more cosine-similar (YF<->OF=0.945, YM<->OM=0.935) than same-age pairs (YF<->YM=0.885, OF<->OM=0.878), a roughly six-point gap that is consistent across all three CLIP variants and is presented as a property of CLIP's pretraining data rather than of the unlearning method or architecture. A strength sweep of Refusal Vector's projection lambda shows a non-monotonic Forget-Accuracy/Redistribution-Score tradeoff: forgetting improves as lambda increases toward 1.0, but redistribution rises with it, and beyond lambda=1.0 over-projection paradoxically restores the original geometry (both FA and RS partially revert), with no operating point evaluated achieving both low FA and low RS simultaneously.
core_contribution: The paper introduces and empirically studies 'bias redistribution' in visual machine unlearning: the phenomenon where forcing a zero-shot CLIP classifier to forget a demographic group does not neutralize that group's representation but instead transfers misclassified probability mass to a geometrically correlated retained group. Using CLIP (ViT-B/32, ViT-L/14, ViT-B/16) as zero-shot classifiers over four intersectional Young x Male demographic groups from CelebA, the authors apply three zero-shot (no-retraining) unlearning methods -- Prompt Erasure, Prompt Reweighting, and Refusal Vector -- to forget the Young Female (YF) group, and measure per-group accuracy shifts, a Demographic Parity Gap, and a newly defined Redistribution Score (RS, mean absolute per-group accuracy shift across retained groups). They also give a geometric argument, grounded in cosine similarities between group mean embeddings, for why redistribution consistently follows gender rather than age boundaries in CLIP's pretrained embedding space, and argue that projection-based (Refusal Vector) unlearning cannot achieve perfect forgetting when forget/retain mean embeddings are nearly collinear (cos(mu_f, mu_r) = 0.929), citing the linear-erasure theory of Belrose et al. (LEACE) as the basis for this impossibility claim.
limitations: The authors state three limitations themselves: (1) only zero-shot (no-retraining) unlearning methods are evaluated -- whether gradient-based approaches such as NegGrad+ or SCRUB show similar redistribution is left as an open question; (2) all experiments use a single dataset (CelebA) and a single fixed forget group (Young Female, the majority/highest-accuracy group) -- generalization to other datasets, group imbalance ratios, and minority forget targets is untested; (3) the demographic attribute space is binary and coarse (age: Young/Old, gender: Male/Female only), so extension to continuous or higher-dimensional intersectional attributes is future work. Additionally (not stated by the authors but evident from the text): no retrain/exact-unlearning baseline is included since the paper argues it is inapplicable to zero-shot methods, so the results characterize only these three specific projection/prompt-level interventions rather than unlearning broadly; and the geometric 'impossibility' argument for Refusal Vector rests on citing Belrose et al.'s LEACE orthogonality condition rather than an independent formal proof within this paper for the CLIP setting.

datasets_used: CelebA (Liu et al. 2015) -- 202,599 images, 40 binary attributes; official test split of 19,962 samples used for evaluation, partitioned into 4 intersectional Young x Male groups: Young Female n=10,331, Young Male n=4,783, Old Female n=1,916, Old Male n=2,932
quantitative_results: Original (pre-unlearning) baseline accuracy on Young Female: ViT-B/32 98.76%, ViT-L/14 97.28%, ViT-B/16 97.22%
Forget Accuracy after unlearning: Prompt Erasure = 0.00% (all 3 models); Prompt Reweighting = 0.00% (all 3 models); Refusal Vector = 64.30% (ViT-B/32), 71.01% (ViT-L/14), 68.35% (ViT-B/16)
Retain Accuracy: Prompt Erasure 78.57% / 80.27% / 79.26% (ViT-B/32 / ViT-L/14 / ViT-B/16); Prompt Reweighting 82.75% / 79.82% / 77.39%; Refusal Vector 33.14% / 38.43% / 36.52%
Delta Acc onto Old Female: Prompt Erasure +71.19pp / +61.12pp / +65.81pp; Prompt Reweighting +69.99pp / +59.60pp / +59.76pp (ViT-B/32 / ViT-L/14 / ViT-B/16)
Delta Acc onto Young Male: Prompt Erasure +0.88pp / +0.61pp / +0.73pp (near zero, all models)
Redistribution Score (RS): Prompt Erasure 24.03 / 20.57 / 22.18; Prompt Reweighting 37.62 / 32.78 / 37.58; Refusal Vector 21.47 / 31.71 / 23.55 (ViT-B/32 / ViT-L/14 / ViT-B/16)

Demographic Parity Gap: Original 0.7298 (ViT-B/32), 0.6075 (ViT-L/14), 0.6690 (ViT-B/16); after Prompt Erasure 0.9697/0.9765/0.9614 (worsens); after Prompt Reweighting 0.9577/0.9614/0.9008 (worsens); after Refusal Vector 0.5335/0.5208/0.5191 (improves, ViT-B/32 0.7298->0.5335)
Group-mean embedding cosine similarities (CLIP ViT-B/32): same-gender pairs YF<->OF=0.945, YM<->OM=0.935; same-age pairs YF<->YM=0.885, OF<->OM=0.878
Forget/retain mean embedding collinearity used to explain Refusal Vector's incomplete forgetting: cos(mu_f, mu_r) = 0.929
Redistribution significance threshold defined as epsilon=2% per-group accuracy shift; observed shifts reach up to 71.19 percentage points, far exceeding this threshold
baselines_compared: Original (unmodified, non-unlearned) CLIP model per variant -- used as the reference point for computing accuracy shifts and DP gap changes; no gradient-based retrain baseline was used since it does not apply to the zero-shot methods studied
Prompt Erasure (PE) -- zero-shot analogue of Gradient Ascent (Graves et al. 2021), zeroes the forget class's text embedding

Prompt Reweighting (PR) -- zero-shot analogue of retain-set fine-tuning (Warnecke et al. 2023, NDSS), redistributes forget embedding mass to retained classes via softmax-weighted cosine similarity before zeroing

Refusal Vector (RV) -- image-embedding projection method inspired by LEACE (Belrose et al. 2023) and refusal-direction ablation in LLMs (Arditi et al. 2024), projects the forget-minus-retain mean direction out of image embeddings at inference
